script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Wednesday — here is your QQQM recap. The QQQ portfolio closed down one point two three percent on the session. Semiconductor names led the damage, geopolitical headlines reset the risk calculus for equity markets broadly, and one chip deal quietly became the story underneath the noise.

The Iran ceasefire is off. President Trump confirmed Wednesday that the agreement has collapsed, sending Brent crude surging more than five percent on the session. For a portfolio weighted heavily toward technology and semiconductors, a return of Middle East risk premium is not a comfortable backdrop — and the price action in these holdings reflected that immediately.

Equity markets sold off broadly on Wednesday as renewed United States-Iran tensions rattled investor sentiment. The Dow Jones Industrial Average logged its worst single session in four weeks according to reporting from Yahoo Finance, with crude's move higher pulling capital toward energy and away from growth. Technology and semiconductor names bore the heaviest weight of the selling. No significant domestic macroeconomic data print crossed the tape Wednesday, meaning geopolitics was the primary driver from open to close.

The headline of the session belongs to the Apple and Broadcom artificial intelligence chip deal — and what it means for the intersection of domestic manufacturing ambition and silicon supply chains. Apple confirmed a major chip-supply agreement with Broadcom as part of its broader effort to increase United States manufacturing, according to reporting from Yahoo Finance citing the deal directly. Broadcom shares responded decisively, closing at three hundred eighty-eight dollars and sixty-nine cents, up three point nine six percent on the session — one of the strongest moves in the entire portfolio Wednesday. The arrangement positions Broadcom as a central node in Apple's artificial intelligence infrastructure buildout, reinforcing the narrative that custom silicon for hyperscalers and major platform companies remains a high-conviction growth avenue. For QQQM holders, the deal carries a dual read: it strengthens the fundamental case for Broadcom's AI chip revenue trajectory while simultaneously signaling that Apple is deepening its vertical integration away from third-party dependency. Yahoo Finance's coverage also noted that Nvidia's relative stock price dropped on the news — a nuance worth tracking, as any rotation of AI chip spend toward custom silicon at Broadcom or Marvell Technology represents a potential headwind for Nvidia's data center dominance over the medium term.

Now to the holdings rundown.

Nvidia closed at two hundred four dollars and twelve cents, up four point three eight percent on the session. That is the strongest absolute performance among the top holdings Wednesday, and it came despite the broader market selloff — a meaningful divergence. The news context here is layered. Alphabet's aggressive artificial intelligence spending plans were cited by Yahoo Finance as directly beneficial to Nvidia, reinforcing the data center demand story. A separate item noted Penguin Computing's stock move was tied to the observation that AI agents require enormous quantities of memory — another tailwind for Nvidia's accelerated computing ecosystem. The one complicating signal: the Apple-Broadcom chip deal prompted commentary that Nvidia's relative position in the AI silicon landscape faces emerging competition from custom chip architectures. Watch Nvidia closely against any further developments in the custom silicon buildout theme.

Turning to Apple, which closed at three hundred thirteen dollars and thirty-nine cents, up zero point two three percent on the session. A quiet price move, but the fundamental news was anything but quiet. The Broadcom chip deal is Apple's story Wednesday. The agreement represents a concrete step in Apple's stated goal of anchoring more of its supply chain inside the United States, and the artificial intelligence dimension — building chips for AI functionality — suggests Apple is accelerating its own silicon independence. The stock's muted gain relative to the deal's strategic significance likely reflects the broader risk-off tone from the Iran headline. Analyst watch here centers on how this deal affects Apple's long-term relationship with Taiwan Semiconductor and whether additional domestic supply agreements follow.

Over at Alphabet, the close was three hundred sixty-one dollars and ninety-two cents, down one point two four percent on the session. Alphabet's AI capital expenditure trajectory was in focus Wednesday, with Yahoo Finance reporting that the company's spending spree on artificial intelligence infrastructure is considered great news for Nvidia — which speaks to just how deep Alphabet's commitment to AI buildout runs. The stock's decline fits the broader communication services sector weakness on a risk-off session. Blue Origin's ten billion dollar fundraise at a one hundred thirty billion dollar valuation, reported by CNBC and cited by Yahoo Finance, adds a peripheral space economy narrative to Alphabet's competitive landscape in cloud and computing adjacent sectors.

Micron Technology closed at nine hundred forty-eight dollars and eighty cents, down three point six five percent on the session. No company-specific news drove Wednesday's move — this was sector pressure, plain and simple. Semiconductor equipment and memory names faced broad headwinds as the geopolitical backdrop deteriorated. The Penguin Computing AI memory story, though not directly tied to Micron in Wednesday's coverage, underscores the long-term demand thesis that remains intact beneath the session's noise.

Microsoft closed at three hundred eighty-three dollars and thirty-four cents, down zero point eight eight percent. News flow Wednesday was light for Microsoft directly, with headlines touching adjacent themes — SpaceX valuation debates occupied some of the analyst bandwidth in the technology adjacent space. Microsoft's move was consistent with the broader large-cap technology drift lower on the session.

Amazon closed at two hundred forty-three dollars and sixty-two cents, down zero point two two percent. A remarkably contained decline for a consumer discretionary and cloud name in a broadly negative session. No specific catalyst drove Amazon's Wednesday move, and its relative outperformance versus peers in the selling pressure is worth noting as a potential signal of institutional support at current levels.

Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred seventeen dollars and forty cents, down six point two eight percent on the session — the second-largest percentage decliner among primary holdings Wednesday. No single company-specific headline drove the move. AMD's performance mirrored the semiconductor sector's sharp reaction to the risk-off environment, and the stock's elevated valuation leaves it more exposed to macro sentiment swings than some of its larger-cap peers.

Tesla closed at three hundred ninety-four dollars and six cents, down six point one two percent Wednesday. Like Advanced Micro Devices, Tesla absorbed one of the steeper single-session losses in the portfolio. The consumer discretionary and electric vehicle backdrop faced dual pressure: geopolitical risk raising energy price uncertainty, and a broad retreat from high-multiple growth names. No Tesla-specific news crossed the tape Wednesday.

Intel closed at one hundred ten dollars and twenty-four cents, down nine point seven nine percent — the largest percentage decline across all primary holdings on Wednesday. No company-specific news was flagged for Intel Wednesday. The move places Intel as the session's clearest outlier to the downside. Given Intel's ongoing restructuring narrative and its sensitivity to both competitive semiconductor dynamics and any macro deterioration, a nearly ten percent single-session drop without a discrete catalyst warrants close monitoring heading into the next session.

Substituting for a secondary slot — Broadcom, given its direct tie to the top story. Broadcom closed at three hundred eighty-eight dollars and sixty-nine cents, up three point nine six percent. The Apple artificial intelligence chip deal was the catalyst, with Yahoo Finance specifically calling out that Broadcom's shares jumped on the news. The custom silicon angle, which also drew comparisons to Marvell Technology in analyst commentary, positions Broadcom as a structural beneficiary of the hyperscaler chip diversification trend.

Three data points to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, any official statement or diplomatic development on United States-Iran relations — with Brent crude already up more than five percent Wednesday, further escalation would compound pressure on the technology-heavy QQQM portfolio. Second, watch for any analyst price target revisions on Intel following its nearly ten percent single-session decline without a news catalyst — an institutional response or absence of one will signal conviction levels on the name. Third, monitor Nvidia's data center order flow commentary from any conference appearances or analyst notes — the Apple-Broadcom deal introduced a custom silicon competition narrative that the market has not yet fully priced relative to Nvidia's forward estimates.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. The key thread to carry into Thursday is whether the Intel selloff finds a floor and whether the Iran situation introduces a sustained risk premium into semiconductor and growth names.
